coredata.dc:title: Detection of Cu2+ ions in aqueous solution via emission quenching of colloidal EuPO4 ultrasmall nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Periša, Jovana
Acković, Lea Lenhardt
Jovanović, Dragana J.
Dramićanin, Miroslav D.
coredata.dc:description: EuPO4 and Eu3+-activated REPO4 (RE = La, Dy, Er) ultrasmall fluorescent nanoparticles (NPs) with an average diameter of 2.1 nm were prepared via colloidal synthesis and tested for sensing Cu2+ and other heavy-metal ions. The pure monoclinic monazite crystal structure (space group P121/n1) of the synthesized particles was confirmed using X-ray diffraction measurements, and transmission electron microscopy images showed round particles with a narrow particle-size distribution. The NPs exhibited intense red emission, which is characteristic of the f-f electronic transition of Eu3+, and the quenching of their emission in the presence of heavy-metal ions was revealed by dispersing colloidal particles in a TRIS buffer and then performing photoluminescence measurements. Strong quenching of the emission (at a rate of 0.195 μM−1) was observed upon the addition of Cu2+ ions over the concentration range of 0–10 μM with a limit of detection of 60 nM for Cu2+. The recovery of nearly 90% of the original emission intensity of the probe was achieved via the addition of ethylendiaminetetraacetic acid and was possible in five quenching/recovery cycles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Colloidal ultrasmall nanoparticles
EuPO4
Eu3+-activated REPO4
Emission quenching
Cu2+ detection
EDTA recovery

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Special Section: Selected Papers from the 5th International Conference on Physics of Optical Materials and Devices (ICOM 2018)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of colloidal EuPO4 and Eu3+-activated REPO4 (RE = La, Dy, Er) NPs
Structure and morphology of colloidal EuPO4 and RE0.5Eu0.5PO4 (RE = La, Dy, Er) USNPs
Detection of heavy-metals from quenching of emission of USNPs
Emission intensity quenching of USNPs in presence of Cu2+
Dynamic and static quenching of emission
Temporal response of emission of EuPO4 USNPs to presence of Cu2+ quencher
Recovery of emission in mixture of colloidal EuPO4 and Cu2+ ions
Quenching of USNPs emission by other heavy-metal ions—Interference study

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of the Republic of Serbia
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministarstvo Prosvete, Nauke i Tehnološkog Razvoja

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support of the Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of the Republic of Serbia (Projects No. 45020 and 172056 ). The authors thank Dr. Krisjanis Smits for his support with the TEM and Dr. Jelena Papan for the XRD measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM image of La0.5Eu0.5PO4 USNPs; b) the particle-size distribution histogram.
a) Quenching of the EuPO4 USNPs emission in the presence of Cu2+; b) Stern–Volmer plots of the Cu2+-induced emission quenching of EuPO4 and RE0.5Eu0.5PO4 (RE = La, Dy, Er) USNPs.

Dependence of the EuPO4 excited-state lifetime on the concentration of the Cu2+ quencher; the lifetime linearly decreases up to a Cu2+ concentration of 32.5 μM (dynamic quenching) and becomes constant (static quenching) at higher Cu2+ concentrations.
Temporal evolution of the emission intensity of EuPO4 USNPs upon the addition of 10 μM of Cu2+ ions.

a) Emission intensities of 1) EuPO4 USNPs, 2) USNPs in the presence of 10 μM of Cu2+ ions, and 3) USNPs in the presence of 10 μM of Cu2+ ions after the addition of EDTA. The inset shows the corresponding emission spectra. b) Integrated emission intensities of EuPO4 USNPs after six cycles of emission quenching by Cu2+ ions and recovery upon the addition of EDTA.
Results of the interference study. Comparison of the magnitudes of emission quenching caused by different metal ions.

Parameters of the Stern–Volmer fit of the emission intensity vs. Cu2+ concentration data for different USNPs: Ksv is the Stern–Volmer constant, σ is the standard deviation of the measurement, and R2 is the goodness-of-fit parameter.
Comparison of fluorescent probes for the detection of Cu2+ ions.
Emission quenching of EuPO4 and La0.5Eu0.5PO4 USNPs in the presence of different heavy-metal ions.

head.ce:title: Detection of Cu2+ ions in aqueous solution via emission quenching of colloidal EuPO4 ultrasmall nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Vinča Institute of Nuclear Sciences, University of Belgrade, P.O. Box 522, 11001, Belgrade, Serbia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: EuPO4 and Eu3+-activated REPO4 (RE = La, Dy, Er) ultrasmall fluorescent nanoparticles (NPs) with an average diameter of 2.1 nm were prepared via colloidal synthesis and tested for sensing Cu2+ and other heavy-metal ions. The pure monoclinic monazite crystal structure (space group P121/n1) of the synthesized particles was confirmed using X-ray diffraction measurements, and transmission electron microscopy images showed round particles with a narrow particle-size distribution. The NPs exhibited intense red emission, which is characteristic of the f-f electronic transition of Eu3+, and the quenching of their emission in the presence of heavy-metal ions was revealed by dispersing colloidal particles in a TRIS buffer and then performing photoluminescence measurements. Strong quenching of the emission (at a rate of 0.195 μM−1) was observed upon the addition of Cu2+ ions over the concentration range of 0–10 μM with a limit of detection of 60 nM for Cu2+. The recovery of nearly 90% of the original emission intensity of the probe was achieved via the addition of ethylendiaminetetraacetic acid and was possible in five quenching/recovery cycles.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, sensors have attracted considerable interest in everyday life owing to their wide range of applications. They are defined as miniaturized devices that deliver real-time and online information regarding the presence of specific compounds or ions in complex samples [1].

Depending on the transduction mechanism, sensors can be classified as optical, electrical, electrochemical, thermal, piezoelectric, mechanical, etc. Optical sensors are based on absorption, reflectivity, light scattering, or fluorescence [2,3]. The first reports of chemical sensing based on fluorescence were published in the 1980s, when calcium indicators were synthesized [4,5].
Since then, there has been continuous growth in the number of scientific papers referring to fluorescence-based chemical sensors and biosensors.

Chemical sensors are used in many fields, such as, aerodynamics, medical research, chemistry, biology, environmental science, and marine life. For instance, they are used for the detection of hazardous materials; metal ions, such as, calcium or potassium, which play a role in biological processes; and H2O2, which can be a product of the decomposition of triacetone triperoxide—an explosive used in terrorist activities [6]. Additionally, they can be used in food quality and safety monitoring [7,8].

The list of analytes for chemical sensing is very extensive (cations, anions, toxic gases, vapors, etc.). To detect these analytes and study their damaging impact on human health and the environment, fast, simple, and low-cost testing techniques with good sensitivity are essential. Fluorescence techniques satisfy these requirements, as they show high sensitivities up to single-molecule detection, do not require reference measurements, and are frequently nondestructive. In addition, the fluorescence detection of an analyte can be achieved using different readout schemes: excited-state lifetime, anisotropy, energy transfer, phosphorescence, or fluorescence emission intensity [9]. The most common technique is the measurement of the fluorescence intensity enhancement or quenching in response to the analyte concentration. Lifetime measurements are often used to describe the mechanism whereby fluorescence quenching occurs [10,11].

Heavy-metal ions are normally found in Earth's crust; however, in cases of their high concentrations in the environment and bioaccumulation, they may be toxic for living organisms. As an example of their harmful effects, mercury can easily pass through biological membranes and cause damage to the endocrine and central nervous systems [12], leading to anemia, reproductive disorders, and cardiovascular disorders [13,14], and cadmium can induce cancer and respiratory system failure [15]. On the other hand, some heavy metals, such as, zinc and copper, are essential for maintaining the metabolism of the human body. The World Health Organization estimates that 800,000 people die annually because of zinc deficiency, with 450,000 of these being children under the age of five [16]. Copper is an essential metal required for the normal functioning of human organs. It plays a role in a bone formation, cellular activities, such as, respiration, enzymatic catalysis, and other biological functions. However, excessive copper intake can cause nausea, hemolysis, and kidney failure and is related to neurodegenerative diseases, such as, Wilson disease and Alzheimer's [17–19]. Contrary to its constructive biological role, copper is also one of the most abundant pollutants, especially in drinking water. According to the United States Environmental Protection Agency, the allowed concentration of copper in drinking water is ∼20 μM [20].

The fluorescent probes developed thus far for selective copper-ion detection are based on organic molecules, such as, rhodamine, 8-hydroxyquinoline, and coumarine. These molecules covalently bind to Cu2+, causing the quenching of their fluorescence emission intensity [21–23]. Different kinds of fluorescent inorganic nanoparticles (NPs)—including conventional semiconductor quantum dots, silicon-based quantum dots, carbon dots, and metal NPs—have been used as sensing platforms [24–26]. Even though these kinds of particles are selective to copper ions, they show disadvantages, such as, broad emission bands, which affect the sensitivity of the probe.

Compared with the aforementioned NPs, lanthanide ion-activated NPs (exhibiting downshifting or upconversion emissions) have well-defined optical transitions, sharp emission bands, excellent photostability, no photoblinking, and long emission lifetimes, which facilitate the simple discrimination of the signal from the autofluorescence of cells and tissues [27–29].

The downshifting emission of lanthanides has only been scarcely explored for the chemical sensing of heavy-metal ions [29–33]. In the present study, four different europium-activated ultrasmall colloidal NPs (USNPs) were synthesized and tested for the fluorescence detection of heavy metals. USNPs are based on a biocompatible phosphate host and Eu3+ as an activating ion (EuPO4, La0.5Eu0.5PO4, Dy0.5Eu0.5PO4, and Er0.5Eu0.5PO4). We aimed to show that in these material systems, the extremely large surface-to-volume ratio of USNPs facilitates the strong quenching of the emission of lanthanides in the presence of heavy-metal ions, which can be exploited for their sensitive detection. For this, we tested the quenching of the fluorescence of these USNPs in the presence of different heavy-metal ions (Cu2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, and Cd2+) and determined the nature of the emission quenching, the limits of detection (LODs), the selectivity, and the recovery of the emission intensity upon the addition of ethylendiaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA). The results show that Eu3+ emission is particularly strongly quenched by Cu2+ ions even in the presence of several other metal ions.

All chemicals: lanthanum(III) nitrate hexahydrate (La(NO3)3*6H2O, 99.99%, Alfa Aesar); dysprosium(III) nitrate pentahydrate (Dy(NO3)3*5H2O, 99.99%, Alfa Aesar); europium(III) nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)3*6H2O, 99.9%, Alfa Aesar); erbium(III) nitrate hydrate (Er(NO3)3 *xH2O, x ≈ 5, 99.9%, Alfa Aesar); ammonium hydrogen phosphate ((NH4)2HPO4, 98.0%, Alfa Aesar); trisodium citrate dihydrate (Na3C6H5O7*2H2O, ≥99%, Sigma–Aldrich); TRIS buffer (NH2C(CH2OH)3, triple-crystallized, 99.9%, Fisher BioReagents); EDTA ((HO2CCH2)2NCH2CH2N(CH2CO2H)2, ≥99%, Sigma–Aldrich); and metal (I)/(II) chloride salts of Ag+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, and Ca2+ (all acquired from Sigma–Aldrich)—were used as received. Milli-Q deionized water (electrical resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm−1) was used as a solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of colloidal EuPO4 and Eu3+-activated REPO4 (RE = La, Dy, Er) NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Colloidal EuPO4, La0.5Eu0.5PO4, Dy0.5Eu0.5PO4, and Er0.5Eu0.5PO4 USNPs were synthesized using the method presented in our previous papers [34,35]. The solution of trisodium citrate dehydrate was added dropwise to a mixture of nitrates La(NO3)3/Dy(NO3)/Er(NO3)3 and an Eu(NO3)3 solution at a stoichiometric ratio (solutions were mixed at the corresponding concentration ratio (50%) of Eu3+ ions to La3+/Dy3+/Er3+ ions up to 100% of Eu3+ which corresponds to the stoichiometric EuPO4) at room temperature. A white precipitate consisting of an Eu3+ (La3+/Dy3+/Er3+)-Cit3- complex was formed, and after the addition of the (NH4)2HPO4 solution, it was completely dissolved by vigorous stirring. After transparent solutions were obtained, the stirring was continued for 60 min at room temperature. The slow growth of particles was accomplished via dialysis against distilled water for 24 h to remove the excess ions. Powder samples were obtained via the evaporation of the aqueous colloidal solutions.

Eu3+ photoluminescence (PL) quenching with different heavy-metal ions was studied after the colloidal USNPs were dispersed in the TRIS buffer (50 mM, pH of 7.4). The total volume of each sample was 2 mL (concentration of USNPs was 0.2 mg/mL). The concentration of the stock solutions of the metal ions used for the experiment was 10−5 M. The heavy-metal ions (Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Cd2+) and other metal ions (Ag+, Mg2+, and Ca2+) used in this experiment were in the form of chloride salts. The metal-ion stock solutions were added in different volumes to the dispersion of NPs in the TRIS buffer to adjust the final measured concentrations.

For the experiments concerning the recovery of the fluorescence emission intensity, a 10−5 M solution of EDTA was used. The prepared solution was added to a mixture of NPs in the TRIS buffer and Cu2+ ions. Each PL spectra was measured 5 min after the addition of the heavy-metal ions.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed using a Rigaku SmartLab diffractometer with Cu-Kα 1, 2 radiation (λ = 0.15405 nm). The diffraction data were recorded with a step size of 0.01° and a counting time of 1°/min over the 2θ range of 10°–90°. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using a Tecnai G20 (FEI) operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. For these measurements, ethanol was used to transfer the powder samples to the grids and ultrasonic bath to separate the agglomerates. An ultrathin carbon film on holey carbon grids (Agar S187-4) was used, owing to the expectation of very small nanocrystals.

Luminescence measurements were performed using a Fluorolog-3 Model FL3-221 spectrofluorometric system (Horiba JobinYvon) by utilizing a 450-W xenon lamp as an excitation source for steady-state emission measurements and a xenon–mercury pulsed lamp for emission decay measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and morphology of colloidal EuPO4 and RE0.5Eu0.5PO4 (RE = La, Dy, Er) USNPs
ce:section.ce:para: XRD patterns of the synthesized EuPO4 and RE0.5Eu0.5PO4 (RE = La, Dy, Er) are shown in the Supporting Information (Fig. S1). REPO4 materials (RE = La, Eu, Dy, Er) crystallize in a pure monoclinic monazite crystal structure with the space group of P121/n1 (ICDD No. 9001652 for EuPO4 and ICDD No. 90001647 for other REPO4 samples). The structural parameters of EuPO4 and all the RE0.5Eu0.5PO4 USNPs were estimated via Rietveld refinement of the XRD data, and the results of the analysis are summarized in Table S1 (Supporting Information).

Eu3+ ions were successfully incorporated into the REPO4 host lattice owing to the same valance and similar ionic radii between Eu3+ and RE3+ = La3+, Dy3+, Er3+ (ionic radius, La3+ = 1.032 Å, Dy3+ = 0.912 Å, Er3+ = 0.89 Å, Eu3+ = 0.947 Å) [36]. The X-ray patterns exhibited broad diffraction peaks. The absence of impurity phases could not be confirmed solely from the XRD measurements, because the broad diffraction peaks may have concealed the diffraction peaks of impurity phases. However, the PL measurements—which are discussed later in the text— revealed no emissions that could be ascribed to Eu3+ located in hosts other than REPO4.

The morphology of the Eu3+-activated REPO4 USNPs was examined using TEM, as shown in Fig. 1 . The representative image (Fig. 1a) shows round ultrasmall particles approximately 2.1 nm in size (when measured from edge to edge) and a narrow size distribution (standard deviation of σd = 0.3 nm, Fig. 1b). The mean particle size roughly equals the crystallite size calculated using XRD, which suggests that each particle consists of a single crystallite. For such small particles, the surface-to-volume ratio is extremely large (2.86 × 109 m−1), as is the percentage of cations (92%), which are located on the surface of the particles and are strongly exposed to the influence of ligands [35,37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of heavy-metals from quenching of emission of USNPs
Emission intensity quenching of USNPs in presence of Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para:
The emission of USNPs was excited by 393-nm radiation (into the 5L6 level of Eu3+), and the spectra were recorded over the range of 580–630 nm Eu3+ is well-known for its strong red luminescence, which is characterized by sharp lines in the emission spectrum [11]. In this spectral range, the Eu3+ ions show two intense emissions: around 590 nm, from the 5D0→7F1 magnetic dipole electronic transition, and around 615 nm, from the 5D0→7F2 hypersensitive electric-dipole electronic transition. The emission from the Eu3+ 5D0→7F2 electronic transition is sensitive to the local symmetry around the Eu3+ ions, and the intensity of this emission is strongly affected by the presence of chemical analytes in the vicinity of the ions [10]. Fig. 2 a shows the quenching of the emission of the EuPO4 colloidal USNPs upon the addition of different concentrations of Cu2+ ions (0–10 μM). The Cu2+ concentration of 5 μM quenched the emission of USNPs by approximately 50%, while the largest concentration of Cu2+ in this experiment (10 μM) reduced the emission intensity by 70%. Fig. 2b presents linear fits of the experimental data (integrated emission intensity vs. quencher concentration) to the modified Stern–Volmer equation A
0
/A = 1 + K sv [Q]. Here, A
0 and A represent the integrated emissions of USNPs in the absence and presence of Cu2+ ions, respectively; K sv is the Stern–Volmer constant; and [Q] is the quencher concentration. A good linear relationship (R2 of 0.994, Table 2
) is observed with no deviation from the linear fit, which indicates that only dynamic quenching occurred in this concentration range of Cu2+ and that all USNPs were equally accessible to the quencher.

The Eu3+ emission quenching in the presence of Cu2+ was also observed with other three synthesized RE0.5Eu0.5PO4 (RE = La, Dy, Er) USNPs (see Fig. S2 in the Supporting Information). The analysis of the Stern–Volmer plots, as shown in Fig. 2b, revealed that the PL of these three USNPs exhibited slightly lower quenching rates for Cu2+ compared with that obtained with EuPO4 USNPs (Table 1). These results revealed that compared with the PL of the Dy0.5Eu0.5PO4 and Er0.5Eu0.5PO4 USNPs, the PL of the EuPO4 and La0.5Eu0.5PO4 USNPs was sensitive to Cu2+ in a concentration range 10 times lower. The exact concentrations of Cu2+ ions that were used for Eu3+ emission intensity quenching for the EuPO4 and RE0.5Eu0.5PO4 (RE = La, Dy, Er) USNPs are presented in Table S2 (Supporting Information).

The LOD was calculated using the formula LOD = 3ϭ/K sv , where ϭ represents the standard deviation of the measurement. The lowest LOD value of 60 nM was obtained for the EuPO4 USNPs. As indicated by Table 2, which presents the LODs of Cu2+ with fluorescent NPs and probes, the EuPO4 USNPs allowed Cu2+ sensing with one of the lowest LODs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic and static quenching of emission
ce:section.ce:para:
In the case of dynamic quenching (collisional quenching) of the luminescence, the value of the excited-state lifetime (τ) decreases with the increase of the quencher concentration, because this kind of quenching leads to the depopulation of the excited state. Then, the excited-state lifetime follows the same dependence on the quencher concentration as an integrated emission, because A
0 /A = τ0/τ (τ0 is the lifetime when no quencher is present). In the static quenching of emission, no change in the lifetime value occurs; thus, τ0/τ = 1 [8]. To elucidate the type of emission quenching, the emission spectra were measured in a wide Cu2+ concentration range (0–85 μM). Both types of emission quenching were detected, as shown in Fig. 3 . Dynamic quenching of EuPO4 USNP emission caused by Cu2+ occurred in the concentration range of 0–32.5 μM, whereas at higher concentrations, the USNP emission was reduced by static quenching. The emission decay curves used for the lifetime calculations and a table showing the lifetime values for each concentration are presented in the Supporting Information (Fig. S3, Table S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temporal response of emission of EuPO4 USNPs to presence of Cu2+ quencher
ce:section.ce:para: The inset in Fig. 4 shows the emission spectra of colloidal EuPO4 measured at different times (from 0 s to 30 min) after the addition of 10 μM of Cu2+ ions. Initially, the emission intensity abruptly decreased. After 5 min, it reached a stable value. Apparently, this time was needed for the formation of the stable and homogeneous mixture of colloidal USNPs and Cu2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery of emission in mixture of colloidal EuPO4 and Cu2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The reusability of a sensor probe is important for practical applications. To test the luminescence intensity recovery after quenching by Cu2+ ions, EDTA was added to the mixture of colloidal USNPs and Cu2+ ions, and the emission of the USNPs was measured. Fig. 5 a compares the emission intensities of USNPs, USNPs in the presence of 10 μM of Cu2+ ions, and USNPs in the presence of 10 μM of Cu2+ ions after the addition of EDTA. The inset in Fig. 5a shows the corresponding emission spectra. We observe that almost 90% of the initial emission intensity was recovered by the addition of EDTA because of the formation of a complex between the EDTA and Cu2+ ions. Further, six cycles of emission quenching and recovery were performed, as shown in Fig. 5b, and it was found that the emission intensity could not be sufficiently recovered after the fifth cycle. The number of quenching/recovery cycles that were performed for the Cu2+/EDTA/EuPO4 USNP system is similar to the number of cycles performed for Cu2+/EDTA with other fluorescent probes [29,32,44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching of USNPs emission by other heavy-metal ions—Interference study
ce:section.ce:para: The emission quenching of USNPs by chloride salts of other heavy-metal ions (Pb2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, and Zn2+) was also tested. The experiments were performed using EuPO4 and La0.5Eu0.5PO4 USNPs, because we previously established that they exhibit significantly higher sensitivity than Dy0.5Eu0.5PO4 and Er0.5Eu0.5PO4 USNPs. The results are shown in Table 3 . The emission spectra confirmed the quenching of the emission. The quenching had a smaller magnitude than that for Cu2+ and a similar concentration range for all heavy-metal ions (see Figs. S4 and S5 in the Supporting Information).

An interference study was performed to determine the selectivity of USNPs toward Cu2+ ions in the presence of Zn2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, and other metal ions, such as, Ag+, Mg2+, and Ca2+. For this, metal ions with a concentration of 10 μM were added, one-by-one every 5 min, to the colloidal EuPO4 solution in the TRIS buffer at room temperature, and the emission intensity of the solutions was measured. As shown in the bar diagram and spectra (inset) of Fig. 6 , all the ions caused the decrease of the emission intensity of the EuPO4 USNPs, but the magnitude of quenching was significantly smaller than that induced by Cu2+. Compared with the other metal ions, Cu2+ shows the strongest quenching effect: >60% of the initial emission intensity of the USNPs is quenched, even in the presence of other metal ions.

Ultrasmall fluorescent europium-activated phosphate-based NPs, such as, EuPO4, La0.50Eu0.50PO4, Dy0.50Eu0.50PO4, and Er0.50Eu0.50PO4, were successfully prepared via citrate-mediated colloidal synthesis. These NPs had an average diameter of 2.1 nm and thus a large surface-to-volume ratio of 2.86 × 109 m−1, so that 92% of the Eu3+ ions were located on the surface of the particles. Almost all the emitting ions on the particle surface of the ultrasmall europium-activated NPs were strongly affected by surrounding ligands. Therefore, when the Eu3+ ions were exposed to the influence of surrounding heavy metals, their emission was strongly quenched by Cu2+ ions with micromolar concentration (LOD = 60 nM), even in the presence of other heavy metals. The addition of EDTA caused the recovery of the Eu3+ emission intensity (up to ∼90%) after quenching by Cu2+, and the process of quenching/recovery could be repeated five times. An interference study showed the selectivity of the USNP emission toward Cu2+ ions in the presence of Zn2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ag+, Mg2+, and Ca2+. The strong emission quenching, together with the selectivity of the EuPO4 probe toward Cu2+ ions and its potential reusability, suggest that these colloidal USNPs are applicable as sensing materials in the environmental and biomedical fields, among others.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support of the Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of the Republic of Serbia (Projects No. 45020 and 172056). The authors thank Dr. Krisjanis Smits for his support with the TEM and Dr. Jelena Papan for the XRD measurements.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2018.12.059.
